title: Scanning and observation
summary: How to read the road early so hazards never surprise you.

sections.heading: Look far ahead
sections.body: Lift your eyes to the furthest point you can see, not the bumper in front. The further you look, the more time you have to plan. Inside town, scan to the next junction or about 12–15 seconds ahead at city speed; on a rural road, scan to the next bend; on a motorway, look 300 m or more ahead. Eyes glued to the car in front is the single biggest cause of late braking and rear-end collisions, and a typical CBR hazard-perception scenario will deliberately put a clue at the far end of the picture.

sections.heading: Mirrors as part of the picture
sections.body: Glance at the inside mirror roughly every 5 to 8 seconds and again before any change of speed or direction. Knowing what is behind you is part of seeing the whole traffic picture, not just the front. Mirror checks before braking matter as much as before overtaking: a too-close follower means you must brake earlier and more gently. A quick over-the-shoulder check (the 'blind-spot check') is mandatory before changing lane, pulling away, or turning across a cycle path. The exam-correct phrasing is 'mirrors, signal, blind spot, manoeuvre'.

sections.heading: Side streets, gaps, and parked cars
sections.body: Look into every side street, driveway and gap between parked cars. Pedestrians and cyclists often emerge from places where there is no warning sign. The gap between two parked vans is a classic 'invisible pedestrian' trap — a child or shopper can step out and you have no time to brake at 30 km/h, let alone 50. Treat every blind spot ahead as 'someone might be in there' and ease off before you arrive, rather than reacting once you see them. Parked cars with their wheels turned out are about to pull out — adjust your line and speed.

sections.heading: Common exam traps
sections.body: Trap one: focusing only on the foreground and missing a hazard at the horizon. Trap two: assuming the absence of pedestrians means a quiet street is safe — children behind parked cars are still possible. Trap three: skipping the blind-spot check before turning right across a cycle path, because the mirror looked clear. Trap four: looking only forward when braking, instead of also checking the mirror so you can brake gently enough for the follower. The hazard-perception part of the exam rewards drivers who name the early cue, not the late one.

sections.heading: The far-mid-near-mirror system
sections.body: Drivers who scan in a repeating pattern miss less. A simple system is far → mid → near → mirror, repeated every few seconds: the horizon for planning, the middle distance for likely movement, the immediate area for current threats, and the mirror for what is catching up. At every junction, add a left-right-left sweep before you cross. This is the kind of 'active scanning' the exam expects. Scenario: at a busy shopping street, far = traffic lights at the next junction, mid = a bus pulling away, near = a pedestrian at the kerb, mirror = a cyclist closing on your right. All four matter at the same time.

title: Vulnerable road users
summary: Cyclists, pedestrians, children and elderly road users you must protect.

sections.heading: Cyclists
sections.body: Cyclists may wobble, ride two abreast, or change direction without indicating. Give them at least 1 metre of side space inside town and 1.5 m where speed is higher, and never overtake just before a bend, a junction, or a parked car they may swerve around. Watch for the head-turn over the shoulder — it almost always precedes a left turn even if the rider does not give a hand signal. Speed pedelecs and fast e-bikes can travel at 40–45 km/h and close the gap behind you much quicker than a normal bicycle. The exam-correct answer to 'cyclist looks over their shoulder' is almost always 'they may turn left'.

sections.heading: Children and the elderly
sections.body: Near schools, playgrounds and care homes, expect sudden movements off the kerb. Children may not look both ways, may chase a ball, and are short enough to be hidden behind a parked car. Elderly walkers may cross slowly or misjudge an approaching car's speed. Reduce speed before you see a problem, not after — the rule is 'see a child, see a problem'. A ball rolling into the road predicts a child running after it; in the exam, that is always the cue to brake, even if no child is yet visible.

sections.heading: Pedestrians on rural roads
sections.body: On a road with no pavement, pedestrians walk on the left, facing oncoming traffic. Joggers and runners do the same. At dusk or in rain they are very hard to spot, especially in dark clothing — dip your headlights and keep well to your side of the road. A pedestrian with a dog on a long lead can suddenly be pulled into the road; a horse and rider deserve extra room and no revving. Slow down before passing, do not flash, and only return to the centre once you can see them shrinking in the mirror.

sections.heading: Mopeds, motorcyclists, and common exam traps
sections.body: Mopeds (snorfiets, bromfiets) and motorcyclists are narrow and easy to miss in a mirror glance — they often sit in the blind spot for several seconds. Add a quick shoulder check before every lane change and every turn across their path. Trap one: assuming a quiet street means no cyclists. Trap two: assuming 'I have priority' means you don't need to slow for vulnerable users. Trap three: confusing a snorfiets (on cycle path, max 25 km/h) with a bromfiets (on the road, max 45 km/h) — their rules differ. As a memory hook: 'narrow, slow, or quiet' all mean 'check twice'.

sections.heading: Scenario walkthrough
sections.body: You are driving at 50 km/h inside town and approach a parked ice-cream van on your right, with children visible nearby. A cyclist is right behind you. What to do: slow well before the van — a child may step out from in front of it without looking. Check the mirror so the cyclist sees your brake lights early. Do not swerve into the oncoming lane to give the van extra clearance unless absolutely needed; instead reduce speed and give space. The CBR-correct answer is to anticipate a hidden pedestrian, not to maintain speed because the road still looks clear.

title: Weather, light and visibility
summary: Adjusting to rain, fog, snow, low sun and night driving.

sections.heading: Rain and wet roads
sections.body: Wet roads roughly double your braking distance. The first rain after a dry spell is the most slippery because dust and oil rise to the surface; this effect lasts roughly 10–15 minutes. Increase following distance and brake earlier and more gently. Aquaplaning becomes a real risk above about 80 km/h with shallow tread or standing water — if it happens, lift off the accelerator, do not brake hard, and steer straight until you feel grip return. Painted markings, manhole covers, and tram rails become particularly slippery in the wet.

sections.heading: Fog and the 200 m / 50 m rule
sections.body: Use dipped headlights in fog, never main beam — main beam reflects off the droplets and blinds you. You may use front fog lights when visibility falls below 200 m, and rear fog lights only when visibility falls below 50 m. Switch rear fog lights off as soon as visibility improves — they dazzle the driver behind you and can hide your brake lights. The exam directly tests the two thresholds: 200 m for front fog lights, 50 m for rear. In dense fog, open the window briefly to listen for traffic you cannot yet see, and use the right-hand white edge line to keep your lane without staring at oncoming lights.

sections.heading: Night and low sun
sections.body: At night, do not outdrive your headlights — your stopping distance must fit inside the beam you can see. Switch from main to dipped beam when an oncoming vehicle is about 400 m away, and use the right-hand edge line as a guide if you are dazzled. Against low sun, slow down: a dirty windscreen turns a glare into a wall, and pedestrians can disappear inside the bright patch. Keep washer fluid topped up and replace tired wiper blades before winter. The exam-correct decision when dazzled is to slow and use the edge line, not to brake hard.

sections.heading: Snow, ice, and common exam traps
sections.body: Snow and ice multiply braking distance many times over; on packed snow, treat any braking as light, early, and in a straight line. Bridges, tunnel exits, and shaded forest roads freeze first, even when the rest of the road looks dry — if the dashboard shows close to 0 °C, treat shadows as potentially icy. Trap one: using rear fog lights when visibility is good. Trap two: thinking main beam helps in fog. Trap three: assuming dry-looking shadowed tarmac is safe in winter. Trap four: keeping the normal following distance in the first rain after a dry spell.

sections.heading: Scenario walkthrough
sections.body: It is a winter morning, the air temperature shown on the dashboard is +2 °C, and you approach a bridge in a shaded valley. The road looks dry. What to do: bridges and shaded patches freeze first, and +2 °C is well within the risk window for surface ice. Slow before the bridge, no sudden braking or steering once on it, and do not accelerate hard coming off the other side. The CBR-correct decision is to treat the bridge as potentially icy and adjust speed early, not to wait until you feel the slip.

title: Reaction, anticipation and safe gaps
summary: Reaction time, the 2-second rule, and how anticipation prevents emergency braking.

sections.heading: Reaction time
sections.body: A healthy alert driver needs about 1 second to react. Tired, distracted or under the influence, reaction time grows quickly — fatigue can have a similar effect to alcohol. At 50 km/h, 1 second is roughly 14 m before you have even started to brake; at 100 km/h, almost 28 m. Phone use is the modern equivalent of drink driving for reaction time: even a hands-free call measurably slows you, which is why texting is so dangerous it is treated as a serious offence with high fines.

sections.heading: The 2-second rule
sections.body: Keep at least 2 seconds gap to the vehicle in front in good conditions. Pick a fixed point ahead (a sign, a tree, a shadow); when the vehicle in front passes it, you should not pass it until you have counted 'one-thousand-and-one, one-thousand-and-two'. Double this in rain, more in snow or fog, and add extra time behind a heavy vehicle whose driver cannot see you in their mirrors. If a tailgater pushes into your gap, slow gradually to recreate the gap in front rather than trying to outrun them. The exam treats the 2-second rule as the baseline, doubled to 4 in wet.

sections.heading: Anticipation over reaction
sections.body: Brake lights ahead, a ball on the pavement, a cyclist looking over their shoulder — these are warnings. Ease off the accelerator and cover the brake before you need it. Good anticipation means you almost never have to brake hard, which saves fuel, brake wear, and the risk of a rear-end collision. Look for clues at every level: body language (a pedestrian's foot at the kerb), vehicle behaviour (a van slowing without indicating may be looking for a number), and environment (a wet shaded patch in winter). 'I expect — I prepare' beats 'I see — I brake'.

sections.heading: Distraction, fatigue, and common traps
sections.body: Anything that pulls your attention off the road shortens the effective gap you have left. A phone call, eating, fiddling with the radio, or an emotional argument all show up as slower reactions in tests. Plan the route and the music before you move off, set the phone to silent or 'driving mode', and break for 15 minutes every two hours on a long trip. Exam trap one: thinking hands-free calls are safe. Trap two: assuming caffeine compensates for sleep loss for more than a short window. Trap three: speeding up to escape a tailgater rather than dropping back.

sections.heading: Scenario walkthrough
sections.body: You are on a wet motorway following a lorry at 80 km/h, with a 2-second gap. A car cuts into the gap between you and the lorry. What to do: the gap is now wet conditions + heavy-vehicle blind spot, so you needed 4 seconds, not 2. With a car in between you need at least 4 seconds to the car. Ease off gently to reopen the gap; do not flash, brake hard, or accelerate to close the gap behind you. The CBR-correct decision is to drop back and re-establish the gap, regardless of what feels 'unfair'.
